prevalence.source: Orphanet; hATTR epidemiology reviews; Brazilian referral center experience
prevalence.trend: increasing diagnosed prevalence due to improved awareness and genetics

incidence.source: hATTR clinical registries and cohort studies

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare) but high per-patient burden (progressive neuropathy/cardiomyopathy)
burden.mortalityRate: Meaningful without disease-modifying therapy; reduced with earlier diagnosis and treatment
burden.source: Clinical registry outcomes; WHO/IHME cardiomyopathy/neuropathy context

riskFactors: family history of neuropathy or cardiomyopathy
age (symptom onset typically mid-life or later)
vulnerablePopulations: patients with HFpEF/cardiomyopathy with red flags (carpal tunnel, neuropathy)
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical suspicion plus genetic confirmation (TTR sequencing); tissue biopsy with amyloid typing when needed; cardiac imaging (echo/MRI) and nuclear scintigraphy protocols where available

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no population screening; cascade family testing when diagnosis established
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Variable; improved with access to specialized centers and disease-modifying therapy

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Referral to neurology/cardiology centers; genetic testing availability uneven across states
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Disease-modifying therapy when accessible (e.g., TTR stabilizers/silencers per local availability), symptomatic management of neuropathy and heart failure, genetic counseling and cascade testing
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Underdiagnosis, limited access to confirmatory diagnostics and high-cost therapies, delays in referral pathways
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Specialty centers increasingly recognize red flags; growing rare disease advocacy and awareness
